event: The Lamentation over the Dead Christ
symbols: limp body of Christ across Mary's lap — the Pieta motif: the Mother who received the infant Word now receives His lifeless body, the total self-emptying of God
Virgin Mary in blue veil — the Mater Dolorosa, the sword piercing her soul as prophesied by Simeon
Mary Magdalene kneeling at Christ's feet — the penitent disciple who anointed His feet in life now mourns at them in death
ladder against the cross — the instrument of the Deposition, the descent from the wood of sacrifice
skull at the base — Golgotha, the Place of the Skull, and Adam's skull in medieval tradition, signifying that Christ's blood redeems the first man
red-robed figure of John — the beloved disciple and faithful witness who remained at the cross
richly dressed figure with turban — Nicodemus or Joseph of Arimathea, the secret disciples revealed in Christ's death
landscape with distant city — Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets, viewed from the hill of sacrifice
crossed hands of the dead Christ — the completed sacrifice, hands that healed and blessed now still in death
description: A wide horizontal panel depicting the dead Christ laid upon a white cloth on the ground at the foot of the cross. The Virgin Mary, in a deep blue mantle, supports Christ's upper body on her lap, her face contorted with grief. A woman in white kneels at Christ's feet, bending low in mourning. A figure in a red robe stands at the left, and a richly-dressed man in a fur-trimmed robe and elaborate turban bends over the body from the right. Several other mourning figures surround the scene, including hooded women. A tall wooden ladder leans against the cross in the background.
theological_description: The Lamentation is the moment of suspended silence between Calvary and Easter — the threshold of Holy Saturday when the God-man lies truly dead and the Church exists in its darkest hour. Rogier van der Weyden's composition gathers the key witnesses of the Passion into a tableau of collective grief that is simultaneously historical narrative and liturgical meditation. The Virgin Mary holding the dead Christ fulfills Simeon's prophecy that a sword would pierce her soul (Luke 2:35); she who said 'Be it done to me according to your word' at the Annunciation now bears the cost of that fiat in full. The Fathers (Ephrem, Romanos the Melodist) composed laments in Mary's voice, seeing her grief as the proper human response to the scandal of the Crucified God. Mary Magdalene at Christ's feet recalls her anointing at Bethany (John 12:1-8), which Jesus Himself interpreted as preparation for His burial — she now completes that prophetic act. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus, who came to Jesus by night (John 3:1-2, 19:38-42), emerge publicly at the cross, embodying the paradox that the death of Christ brings hidden disciples into the light. The skull of Adam at the base of the cross reflects the ancient tradition (Origen, Jerome) that Golgotha was Adam's burial place, so that Christ's blood flowed down upon the first man's bones — the New Adam redeeming the old precisely at the site of humanity's origin. Theologically, the Lamentation proclaims that the Logos remained hypostatically united to the body even in death (Cyril of Alexandria), sanctifying the grave itself and transforming death from a terminus into a passage.

scripture_refs.reason: Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus take the body of Jesus, wrap it in linen with spices — the direct narrative context of the Lamentation.
Matthew's account of the burial: Joseph wrapping the body in clean linen and placing it in his own new tomb, with Mary Magdalene and the other Mary watching.
Luke's account including the women who followed from Galilee observing the burial and preparing spices.
Simeon's prophecy that a sword will pierce Mary's soul — fulfilled in the Lamentation as she holds her dead Son.
The Man of Sorrows, acquainted with grief, wounded for our transgressions — the prophetic backdrop to the entire scene.
Mary's anointing of Jesus at Bethany, which He interpreted as preparation for His burial — Magdalene at His feet completes this prophetic act.
Nicodemus who came to Jesus by night now appears publicly at the cross — the death of Christ draws the hidden disciple into the open.
The messianic psalm of dereliction describing the sufferer's broken body — the prophetic voice behind the crucified Christ now laid to rest.
We were buried with Him through baptism into death — the Lamentation as the sacramental archetype of dying with Christ.
You will not abandon my soul to Sheol nor let your Holy One see corruption — the promise that underlies this scene of death, pointing toward Resurrection.
